title: Graph-theory measures capture weak ergodicity breaking on large quantum systems
authors: Heiko Georg Menzler, Rafał Świętek, Mari Carmen Bañuls, Fabian Heidrich-Meisner
abstract: We study the onset of weak ergodicity violations in closed quantum many-body systems and focus on cases in which they occur through a transition that is controlled by a model parameter. Our analysis is based on representing quantum systems in Fock space and utilizes graph-theoretical measures. As a main result, we show that the recently introduced graph-energy centrality captures known weak ergodicity-breaking transitions via characteristic changes in its distribution. While most numerical tools are limited to small system sizes, our measure can be calculated analytically for large systems of many hundreds of sites and in some cases, even in the thermodynamic limit. We conclude by demonstrating the applicability of our Fock-space based measure to a kinetically constrained quantum model, where we find evidence for a weak ergodicity-breaking transition accompanied by glassy dynamics.
summary: This paper introduces a graph-theory-based measure called Graph-Energy Centrality to detect weak ergodicity breaking in large quantum systems. By analyzing the distribution of this measure, the authors can identify transitions into fractal or glassy regimes in models like the Rosenzweig-Porter and Triangular Lattice Gas. This method bypasses the size limitations of exact diagonalization, allowing for the study of much larger system sizes and the thermodynamic limit.
main_problem: Detecting and characterizing the onset of weak ergodicity-breaking transitions (wEBTs) and the breakdown of the Eigenstate Thermalization Hypothesis (ETH) in large quantum many-body systems.
main_result: The Graph-Energy Centrality (GEC) measure effectively captures wEBTs through characteristic changes in its distribution, providing a tool that works for much larger system sizes than traditional exact diagonalization.
method: The authors use a graph-theoretical approach representing quantum systems in Fock space, employing analytical and semi-analytical methods to calculate GEC moments for large-scale models.
model_or_system: The study utilizes the Rosenzweig-Porter Model (RPM), the Quantum Sun Model (QSM), and a kinetically constrained Triangular Lattice Gas (TLG) model.
key_observables: Graph-Energy Centrality (GEC) distribution, variance of GEC, gap ratio (r_n), and eigenstate entanglement entropy (SEE).
important_parameters: The parameter gamma in RPM, the coupling strength alpha in QSM, and the interaction strength V/t0 in the TLG model.
main_assumptions: The analytical approach for GEC moments assumes the Hamiltonian is scaled to be traceless and of unit variance; some calculations assume small fluctuations in spin positions.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a Fock-space graph of the TLG; Figures 2 and 3 display GEC distributions and variance for RPM and QSM; Figure 5 shows ETH stability via diagonal matrix elements; Figure 6 illustrates the TLG model and its gap ratio/entanglement entropy.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the GEC measure, applies it to the RPM to show transition capture, extends the analysis to the QSM to verify ETH validity, and finally demonstrates its utility in identifying glassy dynamics in the TLG model.
why_interesting: It provides a powerful new diagnostic tool for identifying complex phase transitions like fading ergodicity and glassy dynamics in many-body systems where standard numerical methods fail due to exponential Hilbert space growth.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Fock-space graph of the TLG model (L = 11, N = 5, V = 3 t0) with the GEC values computed for each node corresponding to basis states (see the color bar).

Figure 2: Rosenzweig-Porter model. (a),(b) Distribution of the GEC in the RPM (D = 215) for different values of γ. (c) Analytically obtained var(GEC) for the RPM as a function of γ for different Hilbert-space dimensions D. System sizes range from small ones accessible with ED to huge system sizes and we include results for the thermodynamic limit as well (D →∞). The inset shows a zoomed-in capture of the crossing point region.

Figure 3: Quantum Sun model. (a),(b) Distributions of the GEC in the QSM with N = 4, L = 16 for different values of α obtained from 100 realizations. (c) Variance of the GEC in the QSM across system sizes as a function of α. The variance of the GEC is obtained analytically. The inset zooms in on the crossing point around α = 1.

Figure 4: Triangular lattice gas model. Diagonal ma- trix elements of ˆnL/2 for (a) V = 0.2 t0 and (b) V = 3 t0. (c) Variance of the GEC in the TLG model at half filling (N = L/2) for different system sizes as a function of V calculated using a semianalytical approach (see [84]). The inset shows the system-size dependence of the crossing point V ∗.

Figure 5: Eigenstate expectation values for the observable ˆSz nL ˆSz L in the QSM for (a) α = 1 and (b) α = 1.2. (c),(d) Average and maximal eigenstate-to-eigenstate fluctuations in Eq. (6) as a function of Hilbert-space dimension for (c) α = 1 and (d) α = 1.2. The dashed line denotes the ETH expectation with D−1/2.
